title: Guizotia scabra
taxonomy: Guizotia scabra is classified as a member of the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. It belongs to the genus Guizotia, which includes only one species, Guizotia scabra. The plant's classification is further broken down into the order Asterales, class Magnoliopsida, and clade Angiosperms. This classification indicates its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the same family. Guizotia scabra is native to tropical Africa and is often used as a food crop.
uses: Guizotia scabra is a plant species that has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. Its seeds, in particular, have been found to have antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. The plant is traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal disorders. Additionally, the seeds have been found to have potential anti-cancer properties and are being researched for their possible use in cancer treatment. Further studies are needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Guizotia scabra.

intro: Guizotia scabra, also known as male fern or wild quinoa, is an annual plant native to Southeast Asia and India, belonging to the family Compositae. The plant has been traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, digestive issues, and skin problems, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Guizotia scabra include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Guizotia scabra include decoctions, infusions, and powder, which are used to treat a range of ailments, including respiratory issues and skin conditions.
